methods.description: Procedure for collecting diatom samples from stones in sheltered bays and preparing them for microscopic analysis, including cleaning, mounting, counting, and identification.

steps.description: Five stones with a diameter of at least 10 cm were randomly collected from each site from depths of 20-50 cm.
steps.searchable_summary: collect stones for diatom sampling

steps.description: diatoms were sampled with a toothbrush from a 25 cm 2 surface area of each stone.
steps.searchable_summary: sample diatoms from stone surface

steps.description: The samples were preserved with ethanol in the field.
steps.searchable_summary: preserve diatom samples with ethanol

steps.description: The samples were processed according to the SFS-EN 13946 standard.
steps.searchable_summary: process diatom samples according to SFS-EN 13946 standard

steps.description: Organic material was removed from the samples by boiling with hydrogen peroxide (30% H 2 O 2 ).
steps.searchable_summary: remove organic material from diatom samples using hydrogen peroxide

steps.description: Cleaned diatoms were mounted on slides using Naphrax [25].
steps.searchable_summary: mount cleaned diatoms on microscope slides using Naphrax

steps.description: To ensure an adequate number of counted valves per sample, two slides were prepared for each sample.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare replicate microscope slides for each sample

steps.description: Approximately 300 valves per sample were counted and identified to species level (when possible) with a light [26].
steps.searchable_summary: count and identify diatom valves under light microscope

steps.description: The most recent names of the taxa were verified using AlgaeBase [27].
steps.searchable_summary: verify diatom species names using AlgaeBase database

methods.method_name: Water Sampling and Environmental Data Collection
methods.description: Procedure for collecting water samples for chemical analysis, measuring in-situ parameters, and gathering additional environmental data from existing databases and spatial interpolation.

steps.description: Water samples (0.5 l) were collected simultaneously with diatom sampling
steps.searchable_summary: collect water samples

steps.description: they were used for the analysis of total phosphorus complying to standard SFS-EN 1189.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze water samples for total phosphorus

steps.description: Water temperature and conductivity were measured in situ using Mettler Toledo MX300 probe.
steps.searchable_summary: measure water temperature and conductivity in situ

steps.description: Conductivity was used to calculate the salinity complying the UNE-SCO formula [29].
steps.searchable_summary: calculate salinity from conductivity using UNE-SCO formula

steps.description: The values of total nitrogen, pH, dissolved oxygen and silicon for sampling sites were interpolated from the materials of the Finnish Environment Institute (2013, raster, spatial resolution 20 m).
steps.searchable_summary: interpolate environmental data (N, pH, O2, Si) from raster database
inputs: Finnish Environment Institute materials (2013, raster, 20m resolution)

steps.description: The coast line exposition was also interpolated from the materials of the Finnish Environment Institute (2004, raster, spatial resolution 20 m).
steps.searchable_summary: interpolate coastline exposition data from raster database
inputs: Finnish Environment Institute materials (2004, raster, 20m resolution)

steps.description: July air temperature and July precipitation were calculated using data from the Finnish Meteorological Institute (1981-2010, grid, spatial resolution 10 m) [24] with the Spatial Analyst tools of ArcGIS 10.2.1, Arcmap-applications [30].
steps.searchable_summary: calculate July climate data (air temperature, precipitation) using spatial analysis tools
inputs: Finnish Meteorological Institute data (1981-2010, grid, 10m resolution)

methods.description: Statistical procedures used to analyze the collected diatom community and environmental data, including correlation analysis, spatial autocorrelation assessment, generalized additive modeling, constrained ordination (RDA), variation partitioning, and Mantel tests.

steps.description: Intercorrelations among the explanatory variables were assessed using Spearman's rank correlation coefficient (r s ).
steps.searchable_summary: calculate Spearman's rank correlation matrix for explanatory variables

steps.description: Six of the correlations were diagnosed as strongly correlated (r s < −0.7 or > 0.7, p < 0.001) [31] (see Additional file 2 for details), and thus silicon, latitude, and air temperature were excluded from species richness analyses.
steps.searchable_summary: identify and exclude strongly correlated variables based on thresholds
outputs: reduced set of explanatory variables for species richness analysis

steps.description: Correlograms with Moran's I [32] were constructed to examine the degree of spatial autocorrelation in the environmental variables and diatom species richness. Autocorrelation coefficients were calculated for distance classes with 80 km intervals generating a spatial correlogram with 9 distance classes.
steps.searchable_summary: construct spatial correlograms using Moran's I for environmental variables and species richness
outputs: Moran's I values per distance class

steps.description: The significance of the spatial autocorrelation in the distance classes was tested using the Bonferroni criterion α = 0.05/k, where k is the number of distance classes used [34]. Thus, the corrected level of significance was set at α = 0.05/9 = 0.00556.
steps.searchable_summary: test significance of spatial autocorrelation using Bonferroni correction
outputs: significance flags for each distance class

steps.description: Generalized Additive Models (GAM) were used to study the relationship between species richness and explanatory variables [35,36]. GAMs were fitted using the R statistics package mgcv with maximum degrees of smoothing restricted to 3 [36].
steps.searchable_summary: fit Generalized Additive Models (GAM) for species richness

steps.description: The significance of the GAMs was analyzed using F-tests and Chi square tests [38].
steps.searchable_summary: analyze significance of GAM models using F-tests and Chi square tests

steps.description: Prior to statistical species composition analyses, water quality variables (except pH), climate variables and coast line exposition were log-transformed (log 10 (x)) to reduce their skewed distributions
steps.searchable_summary: log10-transform selected environmental variables

steps.description: diatom species composition data were Hellinger-transformed [39], because this method produces more precise estimates of the percentage of variation explained by the predictor variables [40].
steps.searchable_summary: Hellinger-transform diatom species composition data

steps.description: As a constrained ordination, Redundancy Analysis (RDA) [41] was used to study the effects of climatic, spatial and water physicochemical variables on the diatom composition.
steps.searchable_summary: perform Redundancy Analysis (RDA) on diatom community data
inputs: log-transformed/selected explanatory variables (climatic, spatial, water physicochemical)
outputs: RDA results (eigenvalues, axis loadings, explained variation)

steps.description: Multicollinearity among the variables was assessed by determining variance inflation factors (VIF) [42]
steps.searchable_summary: calculate Variance Inflation Factors (VIF) for explanatory variables

steps.description: values of VIF > 10 were considered to represent high collinearity and thus removed from the analysis [43]. Due to high VIF, latitude was omitted from the RDA analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: remove variables with high VIF (> 10) from RDA analysis
outputs: reduced set of explanatory variables for RDA

steps.description: Furthermore, variation partitioning was conducted to partition the variation in diatom community compositions with respect to all climatic (July air temperature and July precipitation), spatial (exposition, latitude and longitude) and water physicochemical variables (total phosphorus, total nitrogen, pH, salinity, oxygen, silicon and water temperature) and their combined effects [44].
steps.searchable_summary: conduct variation partitioning for diatom community composition
inputs: sets of explanatory variables (climatic, spatial, water physicochemical)
outputs: partitioned variation components (individual and combined effects, unexplained)

steps.description: We then conducted Mantel tests to examine community turnover along geographical gradients [50]. The Mantel test is a method for modeling pairwise community dissimilarities as a function of pairwise spatial distances.
steps.searchable_summary: perform Mantel tests for community turnover along geographical gradients

steps.description: All statistical analyses were conducted in the R environment [45] using packages mgcv [46], ncf [47], rda [48] and vegan [49].
steps.searchable_summary: execute statistical analyses in R environment using specified packages
